facts.text: The blue whale is the largest animal ever known to have lived on Earth, reaching lengths of up to 100 feet and weighing as much as 200 tons.
Octopuses have three hearts — two pump blood to the gills, while the third pumps it to the rest of the body.
Great white sharks can detect a single drop of blood in 25 gallons of water and can sense blood up to 3 miles away.
The Great Barrier Reef off Australia is the largest living structure on Earth, visible from space and stretching over 1,400 miles.
Dolphins sleep with one eye open — literally. They rest one half of their brain at a time so they can continue to surface and breathe.
The Mariana Trench is the deepest known point in the ocean, reaching a depth of about 36,000 feet (nearly 7 miles).
Sea turtles have been around for more than 100 million years, making them older than most dinosaur species.
The box jellyfish is considered the most venomous marine animal in the world; its sting can cause cardiac arrest within minutes.
Lobsters can live for over 100 years and continue to grow throughout their entire lives, never showing signs of aging.
Manta rays have the largest brain-to-body ratio of any cold-blooded fish and can recognize themselves in mirrors.
The giant squid's eye is the size of a dinner plate — about 10 inches in diameter — the largest in the animal kingdom.
Clownfish and sea anemones have a mutualistic relationship: the clownfish gets protection while providing food scraps to the anemone.
Humpback whale songs can travel thousands of miles through the ocean and each population has its own distinct song.
The blue-ringed octopus is one of the most venomous animals in the ocean, carrying enough venom to kill 26 adult humans within minutes.
Coral reefs support approximately 25% of all marine species despite covering less than 1% of the ocean floor.
Bottlenose dolphins can remember the signature whistles of other dolphins they lived with even after 20 years of separation.
Hammerhead sharks have 360-degree vision in the vertical plane, allowing them to see above and below at all times.
The leatherback sea turtle can dive to depths of over 4,000 feet, deeper than most marine mammals.
Anglerfish use a bioluminescent lure attached to their heads to attract prey in the pitch-black deep sea.
The Portuguese man o' war is not actually a jellyfish — it's a siphonophore, a colonial organism made of specialized individual animals.
Horseshoe crab blood is blue due to copper-based hemocyanin and is used by pharmaceutical companies to test for bacterial contamination.
Sperm whales can hold their breath for up to 90 minutes and dive to depths of over 7,000 feet.
The mimic octopus can impersonate over 15 different species, including lionfish, flatfish, and sea snakes.
Tiger sharks are known as the 'garbage cans of the sea' because they eat almost anything, including license plates and tires.
Narwhals have a single long tusk that is actually a tooth — it can grow up to 10 feet long and contains millions of nerve endings.
The immortal jellyfish (Turritopsis dohrnii) can revert to its juvenile polyp stage after reaching maturity, potentially living forever.
Whale sharks are the largest fish in the sea, reaching lengths of 40 feet or more, yet they feed almost exclusively on plankton.
An octopus has about 500 million neurons, with roughly two-thirds of them located in its arms rather than its brain.
Sea otters hold hands while sleeping to prevent drifting apart — groups of otters floating together are called rafts.
The sailfish is the fastest marine animal, capable of swimming at speeds up to 68 mph.
Coral is not a plant — it's an animal. Each coral head is made up of thousands of tiny animals called polyps.
Bull sharks can survive in both salt and fresh water, and have been found as far up the Mississippi River as Illinois.
Electric eels can generate up to 860 volts of electricity — enough to stun a horse.
The colossal squid has rotating hooks on its tentacles and the largest beak of any squid species.
Dolphins have been observed using sponges on their snouts to protect themselves while foraging on the ocean floor.
The ocean produces over 50% of the world's oxygen, primarily through phytoplankton photosynthesis.
Mantis shrimp can punch with the force of a .22 caliber bullet and have the most complex eyes in the animal kingdom with 16 types of color receptors.
A group of jellyfish is called a 'smack,' and some species produce light through bioluminescence.
Greenland sharks can live for over 400 years, making them the longest-lived vertebrates on Earth.
The parrotfish produces up to 200 pounds of sand per year by eating and digesting coral — much of Hawaii's white sand comes from parrotfish.
Orcas (killer whales) are actually the largest member of the dolphin family, not whales.
Seahorses are the only animals in which the male becomes pregnant and gives birth to live young.
The giant Pacific octopus can squeeze through any hole larger than its beak — the only hard part of its body.
Starfish (sea stars) can regenerate lost arms, and some species can regrow an entire body from a single arm.
Loggerhead sea turtles travel up to 9,000 miles on their migration routes between feeding and nesting grounds.
The pistol shrimp can snap its claw so fast it creates a bubble that reaches temperatures nearly as hot as the surface of the sun.
Sperm whales sleep vertically, bobbing at the surface in a position scientists call 'drift diving.'
The Sargasso Sea is the only sea in the world with no land borders — it's defined entirely by ocean currents.
Cuttlefish can change their skin color, pattern, and texture in less than a second for camouflage and communication.
About 80% of ocean life remains undiscovered — scientists estimate there may be 1 to 2 million undiscovered species.
The cookie-cutter shark takes round, cookie-shaped bites out of larger animals, including whales and submarines.
Dolphins can swim at speeds up to 20 mph and can leap as high as 20 feet out of the water.
The vampire squid lives in the oxygen-minimum zone of the deep ocean and feeds on 'marine snow' — dead organic material.
Sea cucumbers can eject their internal organs as a defense mechanism and regenerate them within a few weeks.
Humpback whales create 'bubble nets' to corral fish — they blow bubbles in a circle and then lunge upward through the trapped prey.
The ocean is home to the world's largest waterfall — the Denmark Strait cataract, an underwater waterfall 11,500 feet tall.
A blue whale's heart is about the size of a Volkswagen Beetle and beats only about 8–10 times per minute.
The hagfish can produce a bucketful of slime in less than a minute as a defense against predators.
Eagle rays can leap completely out of the water and are known for their graceful, bird-like swimming.
Hermit crabs form 'vacancy chains' — when one finds a bigger shell, others line up by size to swap shells in sequence.
The frilled shark has been called a 'living fossil' because its appearance has barely changed in 80 million years.
Manatees, sometimes called sea cows, are more closely related to elephants than to any marine mammal.
The deep-sea dragonfish produces red bioluminescent light, invisible to most deep-sea creatures, giving it a searchlight only it can see.
Spinner dolphins can spin up to 7 times in a single leap out of the water.
Whale sharks have about 3,000 tiny teeth, but they don't use them to eat — they filter-feed by swimming with their mouths open.
The moray eel has a second set of jaws in its throat (pharyngeal jaws) that spring forward to pull prey into its esophagus.
Coral bleaching occurs when stressed corals expel the algae living in their tissues, turning white and risking death.
Dolphins have names — each dolphin develops a unique signature whistle that other dolphins use to call them.
The leafy sea dragon, native to Australian waters, is so well camouflaged it looks exactly like floating seaweed.
Giant isopods can grow up to 20 inches long and can survive years without eating in the deep sea.
Stingrays are closely related to sharks — both have skeletons made of cartilage instead of bone.
The Arctic lion's mane jellyfish is the longest known animal in the world, with tentacles that can reach over 120 feet.
Krill are tiny crustaceans that form the base of the Antarctic food web — their total biomass is estimated at 500 million tons.
Beluga whales are called 'canaries of the sea' because of their wide range of vocalizations including clicks, whistles, and chirps.
The coconut octopus carries coconut shell halves to use as portable shelter — one of the few examples of tool use in invertebrates.
The Great White Shark can jump completely out of the water (breaching) — some leaps reach 10 feet in the air.
Moon jellyfish are among the most common jellyfish species and can be found in every ocean on Earth.
Male seahorses have a brood pouch where they carry up to 2,000 eggs until they hatch.
The blue whale can eat up to 4 tons of krill per day during feeding season.
Hawksbill sea turtles eat sponges that are toxic to most other animals, making their flesh potentially poisonous.
Some species of deep-sea fish produce their own light through bioluminescence — about 90% of deep-sea creatures are bioluminescent.
The sunflower sea star has up to 24 arms and can move at the relatively quick speed of 40 inches per minute.
Orcas are one of the most widespread animals on the planet, found in every ocean from the Arctic to the Antarctic.
The peacock mantis shrimp can see UV light, infrared, and polarized light — far beyond human visual capabilities.
The ocean absorbs about 30% of the carbon dioxide produced by humans, making it a crucial carbon sink.
Sea snakes have a special gland under their tongue that filters excess salt from their blood.
The Greenland shark's flesh is toxic and must be fermented for months before it is safe to eat — it becomes a traditional Icelandic dish called hákarl.
Cleaner wrasse fish set up 'cleaning stations' on reefs where larger fish line up to have parasites removed.
The deep ocean is under tremendous pressure — at the bottom of the Mariana Trench, pressure is over 1,000 times atmospheric pressure at sea level.
Sea lions can rotate their hind flippers forward, allowing them to walk on land, unlike true seals.
An octopus can taste with its suckers — each one has chemoreceptors that allow it to taste whatever it touches.
The basking shark is the second-largest fish in the world and filters up to 2,000 tons of water per hour through its gills.
Barnacles are crustaceans, not mollusks, and they are related to crabs and lobsters.
The dumbo octopus lives at extreme depths of up to 23,000 feet and uses ear-like fins to propel itself.
Humpback whales migrate up to 16,000 miles round trip each year, one of the longest migrations of any mammal.
The chambered nautilus has remained virtually unchanged for 500 million years and is considered a living fossil.
Bottlenose dolphins have been observed teaching their young to use tools, passing the behavior from mother to calf.
The ocean covers about 71% of the Earth's surface but more than 80% of it remains unexplored.
Pufferfish contain tetrodotoxin, a poison 1,200 times more lethal than cyanide, yet it's eaten as a delicacy (fugu) in Japan.
Christmas Island's red crab migration sees over 50 million crabs moving from the forest to the coast to breed.
A mother octopus can guard her eggs for months without eating, often dying shortly after the eggs hatch.
The barreleye fish has a transparent head, allowing it to look upward through its skull to spot prey silhouetted against surface light.
Gray whales undertake the longest recorded migration of any mammal — up to 12,000 miles one way.
Electric rays can produce electric shocks of up to 220 volts, which they use to stun prey and defend themselves.
The flamboyant cuttlefish is one of only three known venomous cephalopods and walks along the ocean floor on its arms.
Coral reefs have existed for approximately 500 million years, though modern reef-building corals have been around for about 240 million years.
The ocean's average depth is about 12,100 feet — over 2 miles deep.
Sea sponges are among the simplest multicellular animals, with no organs, nervous system, or digestive system.
Tiger sharks have specially serrated teeth that can cut through sea turtle shells, bones, and even metal.
Comb jellies (ctenophores) move using rows of tiny hair-like cilia and produce some of the most brilliant displays of bioluminescence.
River dolphins, found in the Amazon, Ganges, and Yangtze, are among the most endangered marine mammals.
The wandering albatross has the longest wingspan of any living bird at up to 12 feet and spends most of its life gliding over the ocean.
Hydrothermal vents can reach temperatures of 750°F (400°C), yet unique ecosystems thrive around them.
The wobbegong shark is a master of camouflage, lying flat on the seabed and ambushing prey that swims too close.
The blob fish, which lives at depths of 2,000-4,000 feet, looks perfectly normal underwater — it only appears blob-like on the surface due to decompression.
Marine iguanas of the Galápagos Islands are the only lizards that forage in the sea, diving up to 65 feet to graze on algae.
Some jellyfish are so small they are practically invisible to the naked eye, while others have tentacles over 100 feet long.
Dolphins can echolocate by sending out clicks that bounce off objects, allowing them to 'see' with sound even in murky water.
Oysters can change their sex back and forth multiple times during their lives.
The yeti crab, discovered in 2005 near hydrothermal vents, has hairy arms covered in bacteria that it may farm for food.
Mako sharks are the fastest sharks in the ocean, capable of bursts up to 45 mph.
Over 8 million metric tons of plastic enter the ocean every year, forming massive garbage patches in the Pacific and Atlantic.
The archerfish can spit jets of water with pinpoint accuracy to knock insects off branches above the water.
A blue whale's tongue alone can weigh as much as an elephant.
Mangrove forests serve as critical nursery habitats for many marine species and protect coastlines from storms.
The frogfish can swallow prey up to twice its own size in a strike that takes just 6 milliseconds.
Hammerhead sharks often swim in schools of over 100 during the day but hunt alone at night.
The blanket octopus female can be up to 40,000 times heavier than the male — the most extreme sexual size difference in the animal kingdom.
The sunlit zone (epipelagic zone) extends 200 meters deep and is where most marine life is found.
Walruses use their tusks to haul themselves out of the water and to establish dominance — tusks can grow up to 3 feet long.
The Caribbean reef squid can communicate using rapidly changing body colors and patterns.
Cone snails are among the most venomous creatures in the ocean — their sting can be fatal to humans.
A group of dolphins is called a 'pod' and can contain over 1,000 individuals in super-pods.
The ocean contains about 20 million tons of gold dissolved in seawater, but it's too dilute to extract economically.
Bowhead whales can live over 200 years and have been found with 19th-century harpoon tips embedded in their blubber.
Sea urchins move using tube feet and can right themselves if flipped over using coordinated spine movements.
The great hammerhead shark's wide-set eyes give it better visual range than most other sharks.
Pilot whales live in tight-knit family groups led by a matriarch and follow her decisions on feeding and travel.
The mimic octopus was only discovered in 1998 off the coast of Sulawesi, Indonesia.
Nurse sharks are one of the few shark species that can pump water over their gills while lying still on the ocean floor.
The giant clam can weigh over 500 pounds and live for more than 100 years.
Bottlenose dolphins surf waves for fun, and they've been observed body-surfing on waves created by boats.
The thresher shark uses its extremely long tail — which can be as long as its body — to whip and stun schools of fish.
Kelp forests can grow up to 2 feet per day and provide habitat comparable in importance to tropical rainforests.
Marine Protected Areas (MPAs) cover about 8% of the world's oceans, though scientists recommend at least 30%.
The vampire squid can turn itself inside out, exposing spines on its arms, as a defensive display.
Whale sharks can live for an estimated 100-150 years based on growth rate studies.
The common cuttlefish has W-shaped pupils that give it the ability to see in front of and behind itself simultaneously.
Barnacle geese were once thought to grow from barnacles — medieval Europeans believed this because they never saw the birds' nesting grounds.
Whale songs can be heard over 100 miles away and are thought to play a role in mating and navigation.
Decorator crabs attach sponges, algae, and other organisms to their shells for camouflage.
The goblin shark is sometimes called a 'living fossil' — its lineage dates back about 125 million years.
Manta rays visit 'cleaning stations' on reefs where small fish remove parasites from their skin and gills.
Octopuses can change color in just 200 milliseconds — faster than a human blink.
The Great Pacific Garbage Patch is estimated to be twice the size of Texas and contains about 80,000 tons of plastic.
Deep-sea anglerfish males permanently fuse to females, eventually sharing a circulatory system and becoming parasitic mates.
Corals get most of their nutrients — and their vibrant colors — from symbiotic algae called zooxanthellae.
The spiny lobster migrates in single-file lines of up to 60 individuals across the ocean floor.
Sea otters have the densest fur of any mammal — about 1 million hairs per square inch.
The whale shark's mouth is about 5 feet wide, but its throat is only the size of a quarter.
Dugongs are the sea cows of the Indo-Pacific and are the only strictly marine herbivorous mammal.
The boxer crab holds a small anemone in each claw and uses them like boxing gloves for defense.
Blue whales produce the loudest sound of any animal — their calls can reach 188 decibels, louder than a jet engine.
The longfin mako shark has been found at depths of up to 2,500 feet.
Crown-of-thorns starfish are devastating coral reefs in the Pacific; a single one can eat up to 10 square meters of coral per year.
Hammerhead sharks can sense electrical fields produced by prey buried in sand using ampullae of Lorenzini.
The geoduck (pronounced 'gooey-duck') is a large burrowing clam that can live for over 160 years.
Marine snow — a shower of organic detritus falling from upper waters — is the primary food source for deep-sea creatures.
Spinner dolphins get their name from their acrobatic spinning jumps, which may help remove parasites.
The purple sea urchin population explosion has destroyed 95% of kelp forests along parts of the California coast.
A pod of orcas has been documented hunting blue whales — the largest predator ever hunting the largest prey.
Cleaner shrimp will enter the mouths of moray eels to clean their teeth without being eaten.
The ocean has an average temperature of about 39°F (3.9°C), making most of the deep ocean near freezing.
Napoleon wrasse (humphead wrasse) can live for over 30 years and grow to 6 feet long, and are endangered due to overfishing.
Sponges are the oldest multicellular animals on Earth, with fossil evidence dating back 600 million years.
The flashlight fish has bioluminescent organs below its eyes that it uses to communicate and find prey.
Harp seals are born with white fur that helps them absorb heat from the sun and camouflage on ice.
The vaquita is the world's most endangered marine mammal, with fewer than 10 individuals remaining in Mexico's Gulf of California.
Blue-footed boobies are seabirds whose feet get their blue color from carotenoid pigments in the fish they eat.
Atlantic horseshoe crabs have existed for over 450 million years, predating dinosaurs by at least 200 million years.
Sea slugs called nudibranchs come in incredibly vibrant colors and patterns — over 3,000 species have been described.
The porbeagle shark is one of the few sharks known to play, tossing and chasing objects for fun.
Seagrass meadows can absorb carbon 35 times faster than tropical rainforests, making them vital in the fight against climate change.
The leopard seal is one of Antarctica's top predators, known to hunt penguins, fish, and even other seals.
Barnacles have the longest penis relative to body size of any animal — up to 8 times their body length.
The nine-armed sea star genuinely has nine arms, unlike most sea stars which typically have five.
Coral reefs generate an estimated $375 billion per year in economic value through fishing, tourism, and coastal protection.
The scalloped hammerhead shark was the first shark species to be listed under the U.S. Endangered Species Act.
Dolphins have been observed using dead pufferfish like a ball, gently biting them to seemingly get a mild narcotic effect.
The sunfish (Mola mola) is the heaviest known bony fish, weighing up to 5,000 pounds, and lays more eggs than any other vertebrate.
Japanese spider crabs have a leg span of up to 12 feet, making them the largest living arthropod.
Gray whales feed by rolling on their sides on the ocean floor and filtering sediment through their baleen.
The blue dragon (Glaucus atlanticus) is a tiny sea slug that feeds on Portuguese man o' war and stores their stinging cells for its own defense.
The Weddell seal can dive to 2,000 feet and hold its breath for over 80 minutes.
Whale falls — dead whales that sink to the ocean floor — create entire ecosystems that can sustain life for decades.
Great white sharks are warm-blooded, unlike most fish, which allows them to swim faster and in colder waters.
Remora fish attach to sharks and whales using a modified dorsal fin that acts like a suction cup.
The flying fish can glide through the air for distances of up to 650 feet using wing-like pectoral fins.
Sea pens are colonial marine animals that look like old-fashioned quill pens standing upright on the ocean floor.
The tongue-eating louse replaces a fish's tongue and then feeds on the fish's blood and mucus — one of nature's most bizarre parasites.
Manatees have no natural predators but are frequently injured or killed by boat strikes.
Whale sharks gather in large groups at certain locations — over 400 were once counted together at Mexico's Yucatan Peninsula.
The black swallower fish can consume prey over 10 times its own mass by extending its distensible stomach.
Penguins drink salt water and have a special gland above their eyes that filters out the salt.
The ocean's photic zone (where sunlight reaches) only extends to about 660 feet, yet the ocean averages over 12,000 feet deep.
Female leatherback turtles return to the exact same beach where they were born to lay their eggs.
The coelacanth was thought to be extinct for 65 million years until a living specimen was caught off South Africa in 1938.
Gulper eels have enormous mouths that can open wide enough to swallow prey much larger than themselves.
Coral polyps are related to jellyfish and anemones — they all belong to the phylum Cnidaria.
The striped dolphin can leap up to 23 feet out of the water, higher than any other dolphin species.
Lemon sharks return to their birthplace to give birth, showing natal philopatry similar to sea turtles.
Giant kelp is the fastest-growing organism on Earth, capable of growing up to 2 feet per day.
The giant oarfish is the longest bony fish in the world, reaching lengths of up to 36 feet, and may be the origin of sea serpent legends.
Sea cucumbers breathe through their anus, pulling water in and out to extract oxygen.
Orcas have distinct cultures — different pods have different dialects, hunting techniques, and social structures.
The heart of a shrimp is located in its head.
Sawfish use their saw-like snout (rostrum) to slash through schools of fish and detect electrical signals from prey.
Ocean acidification caused by CO2 absorption threatens shellfish and corals by making it harder to build calcium carbonate structures.
The blue marlin can swim at speeds of up to 50 mph, making it one of the fastest fish in the ocean.
Some sea slugs can photosynthesize after eating algae, incorporating the chloroplasts into their own cells.
Humpback whales do not eat for most of the winter, living off fat reserves stored during the summer feeding season.
The pistol shrimp's snapping claw creates a cavitation bubble that collapses with a flash of light (sonoluminescence).
Whale shark skin can be up to 4 inches thick — the thickest skin of any animal.
Antarctic krill swarms can be so dense they turn the ocean surface red and are visible from space.
Ghost crabs can run up to 10 mph on the beach and make growling sounds by grinding the teeth inside their stomachs.
The dugong was likely the inspiration for ancient legends of mermaids spotted by sailors.
The loneliest whale in the world, known as '52 Blue,' sings at a frequency of 52 Hz — too high for other whale species to hear.
Staghorn coral is one of the fastest-growing coral species and plays a crucial role in reef building.
The harbor porpoise can hear sounds at frequencies up to 150 kHz, far beyond human hearing.
Squid have three hearts — two branchial hearts and one systemic heart — and blue copper-based blood.
Male narwhals joust with their tusks, but the tusk may also serve as a sensory organ detecting water salinity and temperature.
Cormorants are diving seabirds that can reach depths of 145 feet and swim underwater using their feet for propulsion.
The Humboldt squid is known as the 'red devil' because of its aggressive behavior and ability to flash red and white.
Plastic pollution kills over 100,000 marine mammals and 1 million seabirds every year.
Blue crabs can regenerate lost claws, which is why crab fisheries often practice 'declawing' and releasing.
The leopard torpedo ray delivers electric shocks of up to 45 volts to stun its prey.
Dolphins have been trained by the U.S. Navy to detect underwater mines and protect harbors since the 1960s.
Giant tube worms near hydrothermal vents can grow up to 8 feet long and have no mouth, stomach, or eyes.
The Commerson's dolphin, also called the panda dolphin, has striking black-and-white markings.
The red handfish walks along the ocean floor using its modified pectoral fins and is critically endangered in Tasmania.
Right whales got their name because whalers considered them the 'right' whale to hunt — they floated when killed and swam slowly.
Flatworms on coral reefs engage in 'penis fencing' — both hermaphroditic partners try to inseminate the other.
The giant manta ray has a wingspan of up to 29 feet and is filter-feeder that eats only plankton.
Ocean currents transport heat around the globe — the Gulf Stream carries warm water from the Gulf of Mexico to Northwest Europe.
The Irukandji jellyfish is only about 1 centimeter across but its sting can cause brain hemorrhages.
Tuna can retract their fins into grooves on their body to reduce drag, allowing them to swim up to 43 mph.
Swordfish can heat their eyes and brain using a special heating organ, giving them sharper vision when hunting in deep, cold water.
The flamboyant cuttlefish is one of the only cephalopods whose flesh is toxic to eat.
Oyster reefs filter massive amounts of water — a single oyster can filter up to 50 gallons per day.
The Greenland shark is the slowest shark, cruising at about 0.76 mph, yet it successfully hunts seals.
Whale sharks have unique spot patterns, like human fingerprints, that scientists use to identify individuals.
Sea anemones can live for over 80 years and reproduce both sexually and asexually.
The sand tiger shark is the only shark known to gulp air at the surface and hold it in its stomach for buoyancy.
Lanternfish are the most abundant fish in the deep sea and a major component of the deep scattering layer.
The Steller sea lion is the largest of all sea lion species, with males weighing up to 2,500 pounds.
Coral spawning is a synchronized event where entire reef colonies release eggs and sperm on the same night.
The sandbar shark has one of the longest gestation periods of any shark — up to 12 months.
Orcas have been observed teaching hunting techniques specific to their region, like wave-washing seals off ice floes.
The deep-sea hatchetfish has tubular eyes pointing upward to detect the silhouettes of prey above.
Upwelling zones where cold, nutrient-rich water rises to the surface are among the most productive marine ecosystems.
The female blanket octopus is immune to Portuguese man o' war stings and rips off their tentacles to use as weapons.
Seahorses have no teeth or stomach — food passes through them so quickly they must eat almost constantly.
The hammerhead worm is not a marine animal, but the hammerhead shark's head shape is called a 'cephalofoil.'
Beaked whales are the deepest-diving mammals — Cuvier's beaked whale holds the record at 9,816 feet.
Abalone shells are made of a material 3,000 times more fracture-resistant than the mineral it's made of.
The sixgill shark has six gill slits instead of the typical five found in most shark species.
Sea snakes are the most venomous snakes in the world, but they are generally docile and rarely bite humans.
Green sea turtles are named for the green color of their fat, not their shells.
Each year, about 100 million sharks are killed by humans, primarily for their fins.
Rough-toothed dolphins are among the few dolphin species that cooperatively hunt with each other and even with other species.
The stoplight loosejaw is a deep-sea fish that can produce red light invisible to its prey.
The elkhorn coral is one of the most important reef-building species in the Caribbean but is now critically endangered.
Spiny dogfish sharks travel in packs and are one of the most common shark species in the world.
The sea wasp box jellyfish has enough venom to kill 60 adult humans.
A blue whale calf drinks about 100 gallons of its mother's milk per day and gains about 200 pounds daily.
The Scotoplanes (sea pig) is a deep-sea sea cucumber that walks on the ocean floor on tube feet and inhales mud.
Brittle stars can drop an arm when threatened and regenerate it, similar to lizards dropping their tails.
The wrasse fish can change sex — many species start as female and become male as they grow larger.
The bumphead parrotfish sleeps in a cocoon of its own mucus to mask its scent from predators.
Narwhals live year-round in the Arctic and can dive to depths of over 5,000 feet.
Feather stars (crinoids) look like plants but are actually animals that can swim by waving their feathery arms.
The shortfin mako shark must keep swimming to breathe — it cannot pump water over its gills while stationary.
The whitetip reef shark is one of the few sharks that can rest motionless on the ocean floor while still breathing.
Fiddler crabs wave their oversized claw to attract mates and intimidate rivals — if they lose it, the other claw grows large instead.
Sound travels about 4.3 times faster in water than in air, which is why whale calls can travel so far.
Tripod fish stand on three elongated fins on the deep-sea floor, facing into the current to catch drifting food.
The Pinto abalone was the first marine invertebrate listed under Canada's Species at Risk Act.
Olive ridley sea turtles nest in mass synchronized events called 'arribadas,' where thousands come ashore at once.
The wolffish has natural antifreeze in its blood, allowing it to survive in near-freezing Arctic waters.
The ocean makes up 99% of the living space on Earth when you include its depth.
Seabirds like albatrosses can fly for years without touching land, sleeping on the wing over the open ocean.
Reef sharks patrol territories and follow regular routes, returning to the same resting spots daily.
The flamboyant cuttlefish 'walks' along the seabed using its lower arms like legs.
Coconut crabs are the largest land-living arthropod, with a leg span of up to 3 feet, and they can crack coconuts.
The ocean has a 'twilight zone' between 660 and 3,300 feet deep where light fades and bizarre creatures thrive.
Rockfish can live for over 200 years — the rougheye rockfish holds the record at 205 years.
The stonefish is the most venomous fish in the world and can look exactly like a rock on the ocean floor.
A single whale poop event can release a nutrient plume that fertilizes phytoplankton growth across a wide area.
Porcupine pufferfish inflate to nearly twice their normal size and erect sharp spines as a defense.
The megamouth shark was only discovered in 1976 and fewer than 100 specimens have ever been observed.
Anemonefish (clownfish) are all born male but the dominant fish in a group can change sex to become female.
The Southern Ocean around Antarctica contains the most nutrient-rich waters on Earth.
Triggerfish can lock their dorsal spine upright, making them nearly impossible to pull out of the crevices where they hide.
The North Atlantic right whale is one of the most endangered large whales, with fewer than 350 individuals remaining.
Whale watching generates over $2 billion annually worldwide and supports coastal economies.
Sea spiders (pycnogonids) have such small bodies that their organs extend into their legs.
The epaulette shark can walk on land using its fins and survive without oxygen for up to 3 hours.
Deep-sea corals in cold waters grow incredibly slowly — some colonies may be over 4,000 years old.
World Oceans Day is celebrated on June 8 each year to raise awareness about the importance of protecting our oceans.
Shark Awareness Day on July 14 highlights the importance of shark conservation and dispels common myths about these apex predators.
World Sea Turtle Day on June 16 honors the birthday of Dr. Archie Carr, the 'father of sea turtle biology,' and promotes turtle conservation.
World Biodiversity Day on May 22 includes a focus on marine biodiversity — the oceans house an estimated 2.2 million species.
World Wildlife Day on March 3 celebrates marine life, with the 2019 theme being 'Life Below Water.'
World Animal Day on October 4 recognizes all animals including marine life, and promotes ocean conservation efforts.
World Whale Day is celebrated on the third Sunday of February (approx. Feb 19) and originated in Maui, Hawaii in 1980 to honor humpback whales.
World Penguin Day on April 25 coincides with the annual northward migration of Adélie penguins in Antarctica.
World Vegan Day on November 1 highlights the impact of fishing industries on marine ecosystems and oceanic dead zones.
On January 5, 1933, construction began on the Golden Gate Bridge, which spans one of the most important marine habitats on the U.S. Pacific coast.
World Meteorological Day on March 23 acknowledges the role of oceans in weather patterns and the global climate system.
The first Shark Week on Discovery Channel premiered on July 17, 1988, and became the longest-running cable TV programming event.
World Turtle Day on May 23 raises awareness about sea turtles and their habitats worldwide.
August marks the peak season for coral spawning on the Great Barrier Reef, a mass reproduction event triggered by water temperature and moonlight.
Earth Day on April 22 often features campaigns to reduce ocean plastic pollution and protect marine ecosystems.
On October 14, 1977, the Marine Mammal Commission reported that many whale species were in danger of extinction, leading to strengthened protections.
World Environment Day on June 5 has frequently focused on ocean pollution, with the 2018 theme being 'Beat Plastic Pollution.'
The Monterey Bay National Marine Sanctuary was designated on September 18, 1992, protecting one of the most diverse marine ecosystems in the U.S.
On January 23, 1960, Jacques Piccard and Don Walsh descended to the bottom of the Mariana Trench in the bathyscaphe Trieste, reaching 35,814 feet.
On December 5, 1872, the HMS Challenger departed on the first major oceanographic expedition, a 4-year voyage that discovered over 4,000 new species.
World Elephant Day on August 12 links to marine conservation — manatees and dugongs share a common ancestor with elephants.
On July 25, 2021, the first Kunming-Montreal Global Biodiversity Framework session began, targeting 30% ocean protection by 2030.
On March 14, 2013, CITES voted to protect five shark species under international trade regulations for the first time.
On November 16, 1972, the Marine Protection, Research, and Sanctuaries Act was signed into law, establishing the U.S. system of marine sanctuaries.
World Wetlands Day on February 2 highlights coastal wetlands that serve as critical nursery habitats for many marine species.
World Rivers Day on the last Sunday of September (approx. Sep 22) highlights how river health directly impacts marine ecosystems downstream.
The International Whaling Commission's moratorium on commercial whaling took effect on December 14, 1986, saving countless whale lives.
The summer solstice (June 21) marks the longest day of the year in the Northern Hemisphere, when ocean surface temperatures begin to peak, driving marine activity.
On April 16, 2010, the Deepwater Horizon oil rig explosion led to the largest marine oil spill in history, releasing 4.9 million barrels of oil into the Gulf of Mexico.
International Octopus Day on October 8 celebrates octopuses — the date was chosen because they have 8 arms.
Deep-sea fish often have huge eyes relative to their body size to capture as much light as possible.
The chambered nautilus uses jet propulsion to move, squirting water through a siphon.
Manatees eat up to 10-15% of their body weight in aquatic plants every day.
The tasseled wobbegong shark's fringed beard-like lobes help it blend into the reef.
Harlequin shrimp feed exclusively on sea stars, flipping them over and eating their tube feet.
Some deep-sea squid can detach their arms, which continue to glow and wiggle to distract predators.
The world's largest known colony of breeding fish was discovered in Antarctica's Weddell Sea — about 60 million icefish nests.
Crown jellyfish can glow with bioluminescence when disturbed, creating a stunning underwater light show.
Dolphins exhale and inhale very rapidly — a single breath exchange takes only about 0.3 seconds.
Eels in the Sargasso Sea all return to the same spawning grounds thousands of miles from where they live as adults.
The scallop has up to 200 tiny eyes lining its shell, each with a mirror-like structure similar to a telescope.
Blue whales can consume 40 million krill in a single day during peak feeding.
The Dumbo octopus was named after Disney's Dumbo because of its ear-like fins.
Horseshoe crab blood is worth about $60,000 per gallon due to its use in pharmaceutical safety testing.
The ocean floor has mountain ranges taller than anything on land — the Mid-Atlantic Ridge stretches 40,000 miles.
A sea turtle's sex is determined by the temperature of the sand where the egg incubates — warmer nests produce more females.
The deep-sea gulper eel's jaw can unhinge to swallow prey much larger than its own head.
Spinner sharks leap from the water while spinning rapidly along their longitudinal axis to catch schooling fish.
The Mediterranean monk seal is the world's most endangered seal species, with fewer than 700 remaining.
Sea otters wrap themselves in kelp while sleeping to prevent drifting away in the current.
The blue tang (Dory from 'Finding Nemo') can adjust its shade of blue from light to dark depending on its mood.
Giant clams can produce pearls, though they are not as lustrous as oyster pearls.
The bramble shark has thorn-like denticles covering its body, making it feel like a briar patch.
Pilot fish swim alongside sharks not for protection but because they feed on parasites and leftovers from the shark's meals.
The Mariana snailfish is the deepest-living fish ever recorded, found at 26,831 feet in the Mariana Trench.
Ghost sharks (chimaeras) split from other sharks on the evolutionary tree over 400 million years ago.
Sea snakes can absorb up to 33% of their oxygen needs directly through their skin while swimming.
The Greenland shark eats polar bears and reindeer that fall through ice — remains have been found in their stomachs.
Tidal pools are miniature ecosystems that host sea stars, anemones, crabs, and small fish in coastal rock formations.
The wobbegong shark's name comes from an Australian Aboriginal word meaning 'shaggy beard.'
Beluga whales can turn their heads side to side because, unlike most whales, their neck vertebrae are not fused.
Sea butterflies (pteropods) are tiny sea snails that 'fly' through the water using wing-like extensions of their foot.
The shortfin mako shark can leap up to 30 feet out of the water when hooked by fishermen.
Coral reef fish are more colorful than deep-sea fish because color serves for communication and camouflage in well-lit waters.
A single female sunfish can produce up to 300 million eggs at once — more than any other known vertebrate.
The sperm whale's head contains a huge reservoir of waxy oil called spermaceti, which may help regulate buoyancy.
Limpets have teeth made of the strongest biological material ever discovered — stronger than spider silk.
Marine heatwaves are becoming more frequent and intense, devastating kelp forests and coral reefs globally.
The weedy sea dragon is the marine emblem of the state of Victoria, Australia.
The Antarctic icefish is the only known vertebrate that lacks red blood cells and hemoglobin — its blood is translucent.
Manta rays are individually identified by their unique belly spot patterns, like fingerprints.
Remoras can attach to the hulls of ships, and ancient Romans believed they could slow down warships.
The ocean contains 97% of all water on Earth, and less than 5% of it has been explored by humans.
Fangtooth fish have the largest teeth relative to body size of any fish in the ocean.
Spinner dolphins spin in the air to communicate — each spin style may convey different information to the pod.
The longhorn cowfish has small horns protruding from its head and can secrete a toxin when stressed.
Deep-sea sponges made of glass (silica) have fiber-optic properties that scientists are studying for technology applications.
Orcas are apex predators with no natural predators — they even hunt great white sharks for their nutrient-rich livers.
Garden eels live in colonies on sandy ocean floors, swaying in the current to catch passing plankton.
